faq.question: What is the mechanism of Retatrutide?
faq.answer: Retatrutide (LY3437943) is a triple agonist at GLP-1, GIP, and glucagon receptors. In a phase 2 trial (Jastreboff et al., NEJM 2023, PMID 37384082), the 12mg dose produced a mean 24.2% body weight reduction at 48 weeks — the largest pharmacological weight loss result reported in a published clinical trial at the time. The glucagon receptor component increases resting energy expenditure, complementing GLP-1/GIP-mediated appetite suppression.

faq.question: How do I reconstitute Retatrutide?
faq.answer: Add bacteriostatic water slowly down the vial wall. For a 10mg vial: add 2mL BAC water for a 5mg/mL solution, or 5mL for a 2mg/mL solution. Swirl gently until dissolved — do not shake. Store reconstituted vial refrigerated at 2–8°C for up to 30 days. Freeze lyophilized vials for long-term storage.

faq.question: How does Retatrutide compare to Tirzepatide?
faq.answer: Tirzepatide is a dual GLP-1/GIP agonist. Retatrutide adds glucagon receptor agonism to the same scaffold, raising basal metabolic rate in addition to reducing appetite. Phase 2 data showed superior weight loss with Retatrutide over Tirzepatide in comparable time frames. Retatrutide is available in 10mg, 15mg, 20mg, and 30mg; Tirzepatide in 15mg, 30mg, and 60mg.

faq.question: Which Retatrutide size offers the best $/mg?
faq.answer: The 30mg vial ($296.99 after 10% discount) at $9.90/mg offers the lowest per-mg cost — roughly 27% cheaper than the 10mg entry at $134.99 ($13.50/mg). For extended protocols, the 20mg or 30mg vials represent substantial savings.

faq.question: How does Tirzepatide produce weight loss?
faq.answer: Tirzepatide (LY3298176) activates both the GLP-1 receptor — slowing gastric emptying and suppressing appetite via hypothalamic signaling — and the GIP receptor, which potentiates insulin secretion and modulates adipose tissue metabolism. In the SURMOUNT-1 trial (Jastreboff et al., NEJM 2022, PMID 35658024), tirzepatide 15mg produced a mean body weight reduction of 20.9% over 72 weeks.

faq.question: How does Tirzepatide compare to Semaglutide?
faq.answer: Semaglutide acts on the GLP-1 receptor only. Tirzepatide adds co-agonism at the GIP receptor — a distinct incretin pathway. SURMOUNT-1 reported roughly 6–7 percentage points greater weight loss with Tirzepatide 15mg versus semaglutide 2.4mg from STEP 1 (PMID 33567185). The GIP receptor component may also enhance fat oxidation in adipose tissue beyond what GLP-1 alone achieves.

faq.question: How do I reconstitute Tirzepatide?
faq.answer: Add bacteriostatic water slowly down the vial wall. For a 15mg vial: add 3mL BAC water for a 5mg/mL solution. Swirl gently until dissolved, do not shake. Store reconstituted vials at 2–8°C for up to 28 days. Freeze lyophilized vials long-term.

faq.question: Which Tirzepatide size offers best value per mg?
faq.answer: The 60mg vial ($359.99 after discount) at $6.00/mg is the best $/mg — less than half the 15mg entry ($134.99 = $9.00/mg). For multi-vial research protocols the 60mg saves roughly 33% per milligram versus the entry size.

faq.question: How does Semaglutide work?
faq.answer: Semaglutide is a GLP-1 receptor agonist with 94% sequence homology to native GLP-1, modified with a C18 fatty diacid linker extending half-life to approximately 7 days via albumin binding. It slows gastric emptying, reduces glucagon secretion, and suppresses appetite centrally via hypothalamic GLP-1 receptors. In the STEP 1 trial (Wilding et al., NEJM 2021, PMID 33567185), once-weekly 2.4mg semaglutide produced a mean weight loss of 14.9% over 68 weeks.

faq.question: What is the cheapest Semaglutide entry point?
faq.answer: The 3mg vial at $44.99 (after 10% discount) is the lowest-cost entry. Moving to the 30mg vial ($224.99) cuts the per-mg cost roughly in half — from $15.00/mg to $7.50/mg. For extended protocols, the 20mg or 30mg sizes provide meaningful $/mg savings.

faq.question: How do I reconstitute Semaglutide?
faq.answer: Add bacteriostatic water slowly down the vial wall. For a 3mg vial: add 0.6mL BAC water for a 5mg/mL solution, or 1.5mL for a 2mg/mL solution. Swirl gently — never shake. Store reconstituted vials at 2–8°C for up to 28 days. Lyophilized vials store frozen (−20°C) long-term.

faq.question: How does Semaglutide compare to Tirzepatide and Retatrutide?
faq.answer: Semaglutide is the GLP-1-only first-generation benchmark with the most extensive published safety data. Tirzepatide adds GIP receptor agonism and shows greater average weight loss in clinical trials. Retatrutide adds glucagon receptor agonism for the largest weight reductions yet reported. All three are available individually; the Cagri-Sema blend adds amylin-pathway signaling to semaglutide.

faq.question: What is the mechanism of BPC-157?
faq.answer: BPC-157 (Body Protection Compound-157) is a pentadecapeptide sequence derived from gastric juice protein BPC. Research by Sikiric et al. (reviewed in J Physiol Pharmacol 2014) documents effects on angiogenesis, nitric oxide pathways, growth hormone receptor expression, and cytoprotective signaling. It is studied for gastrointestinal mucosal protection, tendon and ligament healing, and muscle repair in animal models.

faq.question: How do I reconstitute BPC-157?
faq.answer: Add bacteriostatic water to the lyophilized 10mg vial. For a 1mg/mL solution, add 10mL BAC water. For a 500mcg/mL solution, add 20mL. Inject water slowly down the vial wall and swirl gently. Reconstituted BPC-157 is stable approximately 30 days refrigerated at 2–8°C. Store lyophilized vials frozen for long-term preservation.

faq.question: What is the difference between BPC-157 capsules and the injectable vial?
faq.answer: BPC-157 Capsules (500mcg × 60) are an oral format intended for gut-specific research protocols — the oral route concentrates the peptide in the gastrointestinal tract before systemic absorption. Injectable lyophilized BPC-157 (10mg) is reconstituted with BAC water and distributed systemically. The two formats serve different research applications and are kept as separate products.

faq.question: Can BPC-157 and TB-500 be combined?
faq.answer: Yes — the BPC-157 + TB-500 blend ships as a single pre-mixed lyophilized vial (5mg+5mg or 10mg+10mg) for protocols using both simultaneously. BPC-157 acts via NO-pathway and angiogenesis mechanisms; TB-500 (Thymosin Beta-4 fragment) promotes actin polymerization and systemic tissue repair — complementary mechanisms that are frequently combined in recovery research.

faq.question: How should BPC-157 be stored?
faq.answer: Lyophilized BPC-157 stores frozen at −20°C for long-term stability. After reconstitution with bacteriostatic water, refrigerate at 2–8°C and use within 30 days. Avoid repeated freeze-thaw cycles on reconstituted solution. Keep away from direct light.

faq.question: What is TB-500?
faq.answer: TB-500 is a synthetic peptide corresponding to the actin-binding domain fragment of Thymosin Beta-4 (amino acids 17-23: Ac-Lys-Thr-Thr-Thr-Lys-Ser-NH2). Thymosin Beta-4 (TMSB4X gene product) is a G-actin sequestering protein that promotes actin polymerization and cell migration. TB-500 research focuses on tissue repair, angiogenesis promotion, inflammatory modulation, and muscle healing in animal models.

faq.question: How do I reconstitute TB-500?
faq.answer: Add bacteriostatic water to the lyophilized 10mg vial. For a 2mg/mL solution: add 5mL BAC water. For a 1mg/mL solution: add 10mL. Inject water slowly down the vial wall — do not shake. Store reconstituted vial at 2–8°C for up to 30 days. Freeze unreconstituted vials for long-term storage.

faq.question: What is the difference between TB-500 and BPC-157?
faq.answer: TB-500 (Thymosin Beta-4 fragment) acts via actin dynamics — promoting cell migration and angiogenesis through actin-binding protein interactions. BPC-157 (pentadecapeptide from gastric protein BPC) acts via NO pathways, growth hormone receptor upregulation, and cytoprotective mechanisms, with particularly well-studied effects on gastrointestinal mucosa and tendon. Distinct molecular mechanisms; often combined in the BPC-157 + TB-500 blend.

faq.question: Is there a pre-mixed BPC-157 + TB-500 blend available?
faq.answer: Yes — the blend ships as a single pre-mixed lyophilized vial in 5mg BPC-157 + 5mg TB-500 or 10mg + 10mg configurations. It reconstitutes with a single BAC water addition, eliminating dual-vial protocols. Individual vials are also available for researchers who need independent dosing control.

name: ACE-031
chemical_name: Activin receptor type IIB ligand trap

name: CJC-1295 No DAC
chemical_name: Modified GHRH analog (CJC-1295 without Drug Affinity Complex)

faq.question: What is CJC-1295 No DAC?
faq.answer: CJC-1295 No DAC (also called Modified GRF 1-29) is a stabilized GHRH analog with four amino acid substitutions (positions 2, 8, 15, 27) that protect against enzymatic degradation without adding the albumin-binding DAC moiety. Half-life is approximately 30 minutes — longer than native Sermorelin (10–20 min) but far shorter than CJC-1295 With DAC (~8 days). It produces pulsatile, physiological-pattern GH release from the pituitary.

faq.question: What is the difference between CJC-1295 No DAC and With DAC?
faq.answer: CJC-1295 No DAC (Modified GRF 1-29) has a ~30-minute half-life producing pulsatile GH release. CJC-1295 With DAC incorporates a lysine-maleimide Drug Affinity Complex that covalently binds serum albumin, extending half-life to approximately 8 days and producing sustained GH elevation rather than a pulse. These are fundamentally different pharmacokinetic profiles and different compounds — not size variants.

faq.question: How do I reconstitute CJC-1295 No DAC?
faq.answer: Add bacteriostatic water to the lyophilized vial. For a 2mg vial: add 2mL BAC water for a 1mg/mL solution. For the 5mg vial: add 5mL for a 1mg/mL solution. Swirl gently. Store reconstituted CJC-1295 at 2–8°C for up to 28 days. Commonly combined with Ipamorelin — a pre-mixed Ipamorelin/CJC-1295 blend is available as a single vial.

faq.question: Which CJC-1295 No DAC size offers best value?
faq.answer: The 10mg vial offers the best $/mg at significantly lower cost per milligram than the 2mg entry. The 5mg is a practical mid-point. For protocols requiring repeated dosing over multiple weeks, moving to the 10mg reduces per-dose cost substantially.

name: CJC-1295 With DAC
chemical_name: GHRH analog with Drug Affinity Complex (extended half-life)

faq.question: What is Ipamorelin's mechanism?
faq.answer: Ipamorelin is a selective ghrelin receptor (GHSR-1a) agonist — a pentapeptide ghrelin mimetic that stimulates pulsatile GH release from the pituitary. Unlike earlier GHRP peptides (GHRP-2, GHRP-6), Ipamorelin does not substantially stimulate cortisol or prolactin secretion at research doses, making it one of the most receptor-selective GH secretagogues studied (Raun et al., Eur J Endocrinol 1998).

faq.question: How does Ipamorelin compare to GHRP-2 and GHRP-6?
faq.answer: GHRP-2 and GHRP-6 are earlier ghrelin mimetics producing strong GH release but with off-target cortisol and prolactin stimulation. Ipamorelin is more receptor-selective at equivalent GH-releasing doses — the primary reason it is preferred in protocols emphasizing GH secretagogue selectivity over maximum GH pulse amplitude.

faq.question: How do I reconstitute Ipamorelin?
faq.answer: Add bacteriostatic water to the lyophilized 10mg vial. For a 1mg/mL solution add 10mL BAC water; for 2mg/mL add 5mL. Swirl gently until dissolved. Store reconstituted vial at 2–8°C for up to 30 days. Ipamorelin is commonly combined with CJC-1295 No DAC — a pre-mixed Ipamorelin/CJC-1295 blend is available as a single vial.

faq.question: Can Ipamorelin be combined with CJC-1295?
faq.answer: Yes — Ipamorelin (GHSR-1a agonist) and CJC-1295 No DAC (GHRH receptor agonist) act on different receptors and produce synergistic pulsatile GH release. The Ipamorelin/CJC-1295 No DAC and Ipamorelin/Tesamorelin blends are available as pre-mixed single vials for simplified dual-peptide protocols.

faq.question: What is Sermorelin?
faq.answer: Sermorelin (GHRH 1-29 NH₂) is a synthetic peptide corresponding to the first 29 amino acids of endogenous growth hormone-releasing hormone. It stimulates pituitary somatotrophs via the GHRH receptor to release GH in a pulsatile pattern that mirrors physiological GH secretion. Sermorelin has a half-life of 10–20 minutes — significantly shorter than CJC-1295 No DAC (~30 min) or CJC-1295 With DAC (~8 days).

faq.question: How does Sermorelin compare to CJC-1295?
faq.answer: Sermorelin (GHRH 1-29) is the native fragment with 10–20 minute half-life producing pulsatile GH. CJC-1295 No DAC (Modified GRF 1-29) has four amino acid substitutions extending half-life to ~30 minutes. CJC-1295 With DAC adds albumin binding via a Drug Affinity Complex extending half-life to ~8 days. These three represent increasing half-life along the same GHRH receptor agonist scaffold.

faq.question: How do I reconstitute Sermorelin 2mg?
faq.answer: Add bacteriostatic water to the 2mg vial. For a 500mcg/mL solution: add 4mL BAC water. For a 1mg/mL solution: add 2mL. Swirl gently until dissolved. Store reconstituted vial at 2–8°C for up to 28 days. Lyophilized vials store frozen at −20°C long-term.

faq.question: Which Sermorelin size offers the best value?
faq.answer: The 10mg vial ($71.99 after discount) at $7.20/mg is the best $/mg — roughly 55% cheaper than the 2mg entry ($16.20/mg). For extended research protocols with regular dosing, the 10mg eliminates the need for frequent reordering and provides the most economical per-dose cost.

faq.question: What is GHK-Cu studied for?
faq.answer: GHK-Cu (Glycyl-L-histidyl-L-lysine copper tripeptide, CAS 49557-75-7) is a naturally occurring copper complex found in human plasma, saliva, and urine. Research by Pickart and Margolina (Sci Rep 2018, PMID 29559024) documents roles in collagen synthesis signaling, wound healing acceleration, antioxidant gene expression (SOD, catalase), and anti-inflammatory pathway modulation. It is one of the most studied skin-active peptides with over 50 years of published research.

faq.question: How do I reconstitute GHK-Cu?
faq.answer: GHK-Cu dissolves readily in bacteriostatic water. For a 1mg/mL solution from the 50mg vial: add 50mL BAC water. For a 2mg/mL solution: add 25mL. The solution may appear faintly blue — the copper coordination causes this color at concentration. Store reconstituted solution at 2–8°C for up to 30 days.

faq.question: What is the difference between the 50mg and 100mg vials?
faq.answer: The 100mg vial ($44.99 after discount) provides a lower effective $/mg than two 50mg purchases. For extended skin research protocols or multi-application use, the 100mg vial eliminates the need for multiple reconstitutions and provides uninterrupted supply from a single lot.

faq.question: Does GHK-Cu pair well with other skin peptides?
faq.answer: GHK-Cu frequently appears alongside SNAP-8 (acetyl octapeptide-3) in research protocols — GHK-Cu addresses collagen and healing mechanisms while SNAP-8 targets neuromuscular signaling. The GLOW Blend includes GHK-Cu as a component alongside other skin-targeted peptides in a pre-mixed formulation.

name: KLOW Blend
chemical_name: Skin & recovery peptide blend with KPV

name: NA-Selank Amidate
chemical_name: N-acetyl Selank amidate — enhanced nootropic form

faq.question: What is Epitalon and what does research show?
faq.answer: Epitalon (Ala-Glu-Asp-Gly, CAS 307297-39-8) is a synthetic tetrapeptide derived from Epithalamin, isolated from bovine pineal gland by Professor Vladimir Khavinson's group at the St. Petersburg Institute of Bioregulation and Gerontology. Research published by Khavinson et al. (Neuroendocrinol Lett 2003) examined Epitalon's ability to stimulate telomerase activity in human somatic cells — the primary basis for ongoing longevity research interest.

faq.question: What is the difference between Epitalon and NA-Epitalon Amidate?
faq.answer: Epitalon is the base Ala-Glu-Asp-Gly tetrapeptide (10mg, $53.99 after discount). NA-Epitalon Amidate carries an N-terminal acetyl group and a C-terminal amide modification that increase enzymatic stability against exopeptidases and may alter pharmacokinetic profile. These are distinct compounds — different molecules, not just modified storage forms.

faq.question: How do I reconstitute Epitalon?
faq.answer: Add bacteriostatic water to the lyophilized vial. For the 10mg vial: add 2mL BAC water for a 5mg/mL solution, or 5mL for a 2mg/mL solution. Swirl gently until fully dissolved. Store reconstituted Epitalon at 2–8°C and use within 30 days. Freeze lyophilized vials for long-term storage.

faq.question: Which Epitalon size offers the best value?
faq.answer: The 50mg vial ($125.99 after discount) at $2.52/mg is significantly cheaper than the 10mg ($53.99 = $5.40/mg) — roughly half the per-mg cost. For multi-cycle protocols where Epitalon is used repeatedly, the 50mg provides substantial savings over multiple 10mg purchases.

faq.question: What is NAD+ and why is it researched for longevity?
faq.answer: NAD+ (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide) is a coenzyme central to cellular energy metabolism, serving as an electron carrier in the mitochondrial electron transport chain. Research interest derives from evidence that NAD+ levels decline with age in mammalian tissues (Yoshino et al., Cell Metab 2011). Downstream effects of NAD+ on sirtuin deacylase activity, PARP-mediated DNA repair, and mitochondrial biogenesis make it a central target in aging biology research.

faq.question: How do I reconstitute NAD+?
faq.answer: Add sterile or bacteriostatic water to the lyophilized NAD+ vial. For the 500mg vial: add 5mL for a 100mg/mL solution. NAD+ is highly water-soluble and dissolves quickly. The solution may appear pale yellow — this is normal. Store reconstituted NAD+ at 2–8°C and use within 30 days; NAD+ degrades in solution, so minimize storage time after reconstitution.

faq.question: What NAD+ sizes are available and which is best value?
faq.answer: NAD+ is available in 500mg ($71.99 after discount) and 1000mg ($107.99). The 1000mg vial at $0.108/mg is approximately 25% cheaper per mg than the 500mg ($0.144/mg). For extended or multi-researcher protocols, the 1000mg provides significant savings.

faq.question: Does NAD+ require special storage conditions?
faq.answer: Lyophilized NAD+ powder is stable stored frozen (−20°C) or refrigerated (2–8°C). Avoid light and repeated freeze-thaw cycles. Once reconstituted in aqueous solution, use within 30 days refrigerated — NAD+ hydrolyzes in solution over time. Amber or foil-wrapped storage is preferred for the solution to minimize photo-degradation.

faq.question: What is MOTS-c?
faq.answer: MOTS-c (16 amino acids) is a mitochondria-derived peptide encoded within the 12S rRNA gene of mitochondrial DNA — the only known peptide directly encoded by the mitochondrial genome in humans. Research by Lee et al. (Cell Metab 2015, PMID 25777562) established MOTS-c as a regulator of metabolic homeostasis, demonstrating effects on insulin resistance, AMPK activation, and longevity signaling in mouse models.

faq.question: How do I reconstitute MOTS-c?
faq.answer: Add bacteriostatic water to the lyophilized MOTS-c vial. For the 10mg vial: add 2mL BAC water for a 5mg/mL solution. Swirl gently until dissolved. Store reconstituted vial refrigerated at 2–8°C for up to 28 days. Store lyophilized vials frozen at −20°C for long-term preservation.

faq.question: What MOTS-c sizes are available?
faq.answer: MOTS-c is available in 10mg ($116.99 after discount), 20mg ($179.99), and 40mg ($269.99). The 40mg vial at $6.75/mg is approximately 42% cheaper per mg than the 10mg entry ($11.70/mg). For extended protocols the 40mg offers significant cost savings.

faq.question: How does MOTS-c differ from SS-31?
faq.answer: Both are mitochondria-targeted compounds with distinct mechanisms. MOTS-c is a 16 amino acid peptide that translocates to the nucleus under metabolic stress and activates AMPK and related transcriptional programs. SS-31 (Elamipretide) is a 4 amino acid synthetic tetrapeptide that concentrates at the inner mitochondrial membrane, stabilizes cardiolipin, and supports electron transport chain efficiency. Different molecular targets, often researched in parallel in longevity protocols.

faq.question: What is SS-31 (Elamipretide)?
faq.answer: SS-31 (D-Arg-2'6'-Dmt-Lys-Phe-NH2), also known as Elamipretide or MTP-131, is a cell-permeable tetrapeptide that selectively concentrates at the inner mitochondrial membrane via interaction with cardiolipin — the signature phospholipid of that membrane. Research by Szeto (Pharm Res 2014) demonstrated that SS-31 stabilizes cristae architecture and preserves electron transport chain efficiency, with applications in mitochondrial disease and aging research.

faq.question: How do I reconstitute SS-31?
faq.answer: Add bacteriostatic water to the lyophilized vial. For the 10mg vial: add 2mL BAC water for a 5mg/mL solution. Swirl gently — do not shake. Store reconstituted SS-31 at 2–8°C for up to 28 days. Freeze lyophilized vials for long-term storage at −20°C.

faq.question: What SS-31 sizes are available?
faq.answer: SS-31 is available in 10mg ($89.99 after discount), 25mg ($161.99), and 50mg ($242.99). The 50mg vial at $4.86/mg is approximately 46% cheaper per mg than the 10mg entry ($9.00/mg). The 25mg is a practical mid-point for medium-scale protocols.

faq.question: How does SS-31 differ from MOTS-c?
faq.answer: SS-31 is a synthetic 4 amino acid tetrapeptide targeting the inner mitochondrial membrane directly to preserve electron transport chain function. MOTS-c is a 16 amino acid naturally occurring mitochondrial peptide that acts as a metabolic signal regulator via AMPK activation and nuclear translocation. SS-31 focuses on bioenergetics and oxidative phosphorylation; MOTS-c focuses on metabolic signaling and insulin sensitivity.

faq.question: What is Thymosin Alpha-1?
faq.answer: Thymosin Alpha-1 (Tα1, CAS 62304-98-7) is a 28 amino acid polypeptide derived from thymosin fraction 5, originally isolated from bovine thymus by Goldstein et al. Research documents effects on T-cell maturation, dendritic cell function, natural killer cell activity, and antiviral immune responses. Thymalfasin — the synthetic form of Tα1 — is approved in several countries for treatment of hepatitis B, hepatitis C, and as a vaccine adjuvant.

faq.question: How do I reconstitute Thymosin Alpha-1?
faq.answer: Add bacteriostatic water to the lyophilized vial. For the 5mg vial: add 1mL BAC water for a 5mg/mL solution. Thymosin Alpha-1 dissolves readily — the solution should be clear. Store reconstituted vial at 2–8°C for up to 28 days. Freeze lyophilized vials for long-term storage.

faq.question: How does Thymosin Alpha-1 differ from the Khavinson thymic peptides?
faq.answer: Thymosin Alpha-1 is a 28 amino acid polypeptide from a US-developed thymic fraction isolation program (Goldstein, 1970s). Vilon (Lys-Glu) and Thymogen (Glu-Trp) are 2–3 amino acid Khavinson bioregulators from Soviet-era thymic peptide research — much shorter sequences with distinct mechanisms from a separate research tradition. Not interchangeable compounds.

faq.question: What is the pricing difference between 5mg and 10mg Thymosin Alpha-1?
faq.answer: The 5mg vial is $31.49 after discount; the 10mg is $62.99. The per-mg cost is equivalent between the two sizes. The 10mg is practical for protocols requiring ≥5mg per cycle, reducing the number of vials handled per study period.

faq.question: What is PT-141 (Bremelanotide)?
faq.answer: PT-141 (Bremelanotide, CAS 189691-06-3) is a cyclic heptapeptide melanocortin receptor agonist derived from Melanotan II. It preferentially activates MC4R in the hypothalamus — a pathway involved in central nervous system regulation of sexual arousal and motivation. Unlike PDE5 inhibitors, PT-141 acts centrally via melanocortin signaling rather than peripherally via vascular mechanisms. FDA-approved (Vyleesi) for hypoactive sexual desire disorder in premenopausal women.

faq.question: How does PT-141 differ from Melanotan II?
faq.answer: PT-141 is a metabolite and derivative of Melanotan II with the C-terminal amino acid removed, creating a more MC4R-selective pharmacological profile. Melanotan II retains broader melanocortin receptor activity (MC1R through MC5R) including melanogenic effects. PT-141 retains the MC4R-mediated arousal pathway with reduced melanogenic activity at research doses — a more targeted mechanism.

faq.question: How do I reconstitute PT-141?
faq.answer: Add bacteriostatic water to the lyophilized 10mg vial. For a 2mg/mL solution: add 5mL BAC water. For a 1mg/mL solution: add 10mL. Swirl gently until dissolved. Store reconstituted PT-141 at 2–8°C for up to 28 days, protected from light. Available in a single 10mg vial ($80.99 after discount).

faq.question: What is Kisspeptin-10 and what does it regulate?
faq.answer: Kisspeptin-10 (Metastin 45-54, CAS 374675-21-5) is the 10 amino acid C-terminal fragment of the KISS1-encoded protein Kisspeptin-54 — the most potent naturally occurring kisspeptin fragment at the KISS1R (GPR54) receptor. Kisspeptin neurons in the hypothalamic arcuate and anteroventral periventricular nuclei drive pulsatile GnRH secretion, making Kisspeptin-10 the primary upstream regulator of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis.

faq.question: How does Kisspeptin-10 relate to Gonadorelin?
faq.answer: Kisspeptin-10 acts on KISS1R receptors on GnRH neurons to trigger GnRH (Gonadorelin) release. Gonadorelin then acts on pituitary gonadotrophs to stimulate LH and FSH secretion. Kisspeptin-10 is one signaling level upstream of Gonadorelin in the HPG axis — together they allow researchers to dissect hypothalamic versus pituitary regulation of the reproductive axis.

faq.question: How do I reconstitute Kisspeptin-10?
faq.answer: Add bacteriostatic water to the lyophilized 5mg vial. For a 1mg/mL solution: add 5mL BAC water. For a 0.5mg/mL solution: add 10mL. Swirl gently until dissolved. Store reconstituted Kisspeptin-10 at 2–8°C for up to 28 days. Freeze lyophilized vials for long-term storage at −20°C.

faq.question: What other reproductive axis peptides are available?
faq.answer: Gonadorelin (synthetic GnRH) acts directly on pituitary gonadotrophs. Oxytocin operates downstream in social bonding and peripheral reproductive physiology. PT-141 (bremelanotide) acts on melanocortin MC4R pathways involved in arousal — a distinct mechanism from the HPG axis. Together these compounds allow multi-level investigation of reproductive and social neuroendocrinology.

faq.question: What is BAM-15?
faq.answer: BAM-15 is a mitochondrial protonophore (proton carrier) that uncouples the electron transport chain from ATP synthesis by shuttling protons across the inner mitochondrial membrane, dissipating membrane potential as heat. Research by Kenwood et al. (Cell Rep 2014) demonstrated that BAM-15 increases energy expenditure in obese mice without the cardiac toxicity or hyperthermia observed with older uncouplers like 2,4-DNP. Available as a pre-dissolved liquid solution — no reconstitution required.

faq.question: What is the difference between the two BAM-15 concentrations?
faq.answer: BAM-15 is available in two distinct liquid formulations: 30mg/mL in a 30mL bottle ($80.99 after discount, = 900mg total) and 50mg/mL in a 30mL bottle ($116.99, = 1500mg total). These are different concentration formulations — the 50mg/mL provides 67% more total compound at a lower $/mg. Choose based on the concentration required for your specific research protocol.

faq.question: Does BAM-15 require reconstitution?
faq.answer: No — BAM-15 ships as a pre-dissolved liquid solution and requires no reconstitution with BAC water. Store at 2–8°C refrigerated. Do not freeze the liquid formulation as this can affect solubility. Protect from light and use within the labeled expiration period.

faq.question: How does BAM-15 compare to AICAR mechanistically?
faq.answer: BAM-15 directly uncouples mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation by proton shuttling — increasing absolute energy expenditure via heat dissipation. AICAR activates AMPK signaling to modulate metabolic gene expression and enzyme activity. BAM-15 studies direct mitochondrial uncoupling; AICAR studies the upstream regulatory kinase layer. They address different questions in energy metabolism and are complementary research tools.

faq.question: What is AICAR and how does it activate AMPK?
faq.answer: AICAR (5-Aminoimidazole-4-carboxamide ribonucleotide, CAS 2627-69-2) is taken up by cells via adenosine transporters and phosphorylated by adenosine kinase to form ZMP — a structural AMP mimic. ZMP allosterically activates AMPK (AMP-activated protein kinase) by binding the γ subunit regulatory domain, mimicking cellular energy depletion without requiring actual ATP reduction. This makes AICAR a precise pharmacological tool for studying AMPK-dependent metabolic pathways in isolation.

faq.question: What does AMPK activation do in research models?
faq.answer: AMPK is the master metabolic kinase that promotes glucose uptake (GLUT4 translocation), fatty acid oxidation (via ACC inhibition), mitochondrial biogenesis (via PGC-1α activation), and autophagy. AICAR-mediated AMPK activation in cell and animal studies is used to model the metabolic state of exercise and caloric restriction without physical intervention — a core research tool in metabolic biology.

faq.question: How do I reconstitute AICAR?
faq.answer: Add sterile or bacteriostatic water to the lyophilized 50mg vial. For a 10mg/mL solution: add 5mL. AICAR is highly water-soluble and dissolves rapidly. The solution should be clear. Store reconstituted AICAR at 2–8°C for up to 14 days. Store lyophilized powder frozen (−20°C) for long-term stability.

faq.question: How does AICAR compare to BAM-15 as a metabolic research compound?
faq.answer: AICAR activates AMPK signaling — the regulatory kinase controlling metabolic gene expression and substrate utilization. BAM-15 is a protonophore that directly uncouples mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation, increasing oxygen consumption as heat. AICAR studies metabolic signaling regulation; BAM-15 studies direct mitochondrial energy dissipation. Complementary mechanisms for different questions in energy metabolism research.

faq.question: What is Glutathione and what is it used for in research?
faq.answer: Glutathione (L-γ-glutamyl-L-cysteinylglycine, CAS 70-18-8) is the primary endogenous antioxidant tripeptide, synthesized in virtually all mammalian cells from glutamate, cysteine, and glycine. It maintains cellular redox homeostasis, supports detoxification via glutathione S-transferase conjugation, participates in immune function, and acts as a cofactor for antioxidant enzymes. Research applications include oxidative stress models, liver protection studies, and immune function investigation.

faq.question: Which Glutathione size offers the best value?
faq.answer: The 1500mg vial ($96.99 after discount) at $0.065/mg is roughly 65% cheaper per mg than the 200mg entry ($40.49 = $0.202/mg). For extended oxidative stress protocols or multi-researcher use, the 1500mg is far the most economical option.

faq.question: How do I reconstitute Glutathione?
faq.answer: Add bacteriostatic water to the lyophilized vial. For a 50mg/mL solution from the 200mg vial: add 4mL BAC water. Glutathione dissolves readily. The solution should be clear to faintly yellow. Store reconstituted glutathione at 2–8°C and use within 14 days — glutathione degrades faster than most peptides due to disulfide bond oxidation.

faq.question: Does Glutathione degrade quickly in solution?
faq.answer: Yes — glutathione in aqueous solution is susceptible to oxidation of the cysteine thiol group, forming glutathione disulfide (GSSG). Use reconstituted solution within 14 days refrigerated. Lyophilized powder is significantly more stable; store unreconstituted vials frozen or refrigerated and keep sealed until use to maximize shelf life.

name: RU-58841
chemical_name: Non-steroidal androgen receptor antagonist topical solution
